ideas.headline: COI drafting is the cleanest EU-AI-Act-safe wedge in the ministry, and Commission high-risk guidance now explicitly carves preparatory translation/summarisation OUT of the high-risk regime. Conditional only because the precedent stack (SEM internal pilot 2025-2026, BAMF MILo-as-LLM) is softer on re-verification than the original memo implied; the design path is clear, the precedent footing is what
blockers: Refugee-law co-founder must be signed before the first ministry conversation; pitching without that credibility shield is a wasted shot.
UNHCR Greece briefing must precede ministry signature, not follow it.
Source whitelist must be published openly at contract signature; any whisper of a closed/opaque source list ends the political cover.
ideas.next30days: Identify and meet three candidate refugee-law co-founders (Asylum Service alumni, GCR veterans, refugee-law academics) in Athens. · Read the Commission's February 2026 high-risk guidance on the preparatory-task carve-out end-to-end; draft the legal-positioning memo that frames COI drafting as preparatory.

ideas.headline: Operational triage with hard prohibitions on nationality/religion/ethnicity as routing inputs is the safest fundamental-rights posture in the sweep; political tailwind on residence-permit backlog is durable across governments. Pass, with the UK Streaming Tool history baked into the contract as a fairness-audit commitment.
blockers: Incumbent SI (Intracom/Byte) API access for the underlying case-management substrate. Without it the agent layer cannot read state. Mitigation path: pitch the Digital Governance ministry as the inter-agency-API broker.

Streaming Tool history must be addressed in the proposal, not glossed: published rule set, hard-banned routing inputs, independent fairness audit at month 3 and month 12, Hellenic Data Protection Authority review.
Greek-document OCR quality on aged or low-quality scans is the one technical watch-item.
ideas.next30days: Map Law 4251/2014 and successor instruments into a rule-engine spec for at least three permit categories (employment, family reunification, renewals). · Identify and meet two immigration-law co-founder candidates (former Migration Policy Directorate, or 4251-fluent practitioners).

ideas.headline: Single-decision-maker buyer, no fundamental-rights surface, fast to demo, and the UK precedent has matured: the Redbox-to-Assist transition shows departmental specialisations win where horizontal chatbots stalled. Migration-specific source integrations (Alkyoni, EUAA, Coast Guard) ARE the moat. Pass, fastest pilot in the sweep.
blockers: Digital Governance ministry launching a horizontal Greek-equivalent of Albert/Redbox before the migration-specific moat is dug deep. Speed-to-deploy is the only mitigation.
Press office is small and unlikely to drive a competitive procurement on its own; need the minister's chief of staff to bless an apevtheias anathesi at the €30-60k tier.
Disclosure question: ministry may want zero public disclosure of agent use, which is fine for the tool but constrains studio's ability to market the deployment.
ideas.next30days: Identify former Ministry of Migration & Asylum press-office staffer or parliamentary-affairs official to advise. · Map the source registry: Alkyoni public statistics, Coast Guard arrival data, Eurostat migration tables, EUAA dashboards, Διαύγεια published acts. List which require integrations vs. scraping.

ideas.headline: EU AI Act preparatory-task carve-out is helpful but stacks against unverifiable Migrationsverket/IND precedent (axis at 2), GDPR Article 9 consent-flow burden, low-resource-language ASR quality floor, and capital intensity that pushes the pilot tier above the apevtheias-anathesi sweet spot. Originally promoted in the source set, but the qualification fail is driven by precedent freshness collapse
blockers: GDPR Article 9 consent flow in the applicant's source language, with documented free-and-informed consent, is the rate-limiting step.
Tigrinya / Pashto / Sudanese-Arabic / southern-Afghan-Dari ASR quality floor must be measured BEFORE the political pitch; shipping below-threshold confidence creates ECHR exposure.
Greek sovereign cloud / on-prem deployment cost stack pushes capital efficiency below the pilot tier the rest of the sweep operates in.
Migrationsverket / IND precedent re-verification in Swedish / Dutch sources before the memo is sent to the Secretary General; the originally cited precedents are softer than implied.
ideas.next30days: Run an ASR-quality benchmark on Tigrinya, Pashto, Dari, and Sudanese Arabic against a public test set; publish a per-language confidence floor internally before any ministry conversation. · Get Migrationsverket and IND precedent verification through Swedish-language and Dutch-language sources, or direct contact with the agencies. Stop citing them as 'confirmed operational' until verified.

ideas.headline: Original kill stands. EU AI Act Annex III Section 7(d) is written for exactly this product; the December 2027 enforcement date is a delay, not a reprieve; and the press-cycle dynamics around 'AI denied this family asylum' are unmanageable for a Greek venture studio. Kill, do not re-open within the 2026-2027 horizon.
blockers: EU AI Act Annex III Section 7(d) — high-risk classification applied at the point of intended use; full compliance regime from 2 December 2027 with significant pre-enforcement scrutiny already from Commission and supervisory authorities.
Political third rail — UNHCR, NGO sector, Greek Ombudsman, National Commission for Human Rights, and ECHR petitioners all in the litigation chain.
No founder team can carry the public credibility burden in the current political environment.
ideas.next30days: Do not engage. Allocate the team's attention to the four passes and the one conditional. · Set a 24-month calendar reminder to re-examine ONLY if (a) another member state ships a merits-drafting deployment that survives an ECHR challenge AND (b) Greek FRIA tooling and Commission high-risk guidance has matured into a defensible compliance posture. Neither condition is in sight.
